summary: Atwood explores the challenge of storing private encryption keys when both encryption and decryption happen on the same machine. While cross-machine scenarios are straightforward—keep the private key on a server, distribute public keys to clients—local-only encryption presents a fundamental security problem: users have physical control of their machines. He shares a reader's detailed response outlining storage options (machine store, user store, registry, smart cards) and warns against storing keys in regular strings due to .NET's garbage collection. His conclusion is sobering: true security requires keeping private keys on separate hardware, and local storage can only make key discovery 'inconvenient,' not impossible.
key_points: Cross-machine encryption is inherently more secure because the private key never leaves the server
When both keys must exist on the same machine, complete security is theoretically impossible since users have physical control
Storage options include machine key store, user key store, registry with ACLs, or smart cards—each with different security tradeoffs
Never store keys in regular .NET strings because garbage collection doesn't properly clear them from memory
Hardware-based solutions like smart cards or embedded keys (Xbox 360 style) provide the strongest local protection
money_quotes: I had the luxury of keeping my private key on a different physical server. I had never even considered the problem of private key storage on the same machine!
How in the world do you keep the private key away from the prying eyes of the local user?
It seems like an insoluble problem to me, since users have complete physical control over their own machines.
Remember, never store an instance of your keys or passwords in a string! The .NET GC does not clear these values very well, and they are visible in the string table memory for anyone to steal!
The only way to keep a private key truly private is to store it on a completely different machine.
If you must store the private key on the same machine, some vulnerability is inevitable. You can only make it inconvenient for a user to find the private key through software protection.
This is another reason why the user comments are the best part of a blog.
key_insight: Local private key storage is fundamentally a losing battle against determined users with physical machine access—you can only raise the difficulty bar, never eliminate the vulnerability.
